title: Scrolling Mnemonics
text: Scrolling Mnemonics The scrolling mnemonics are used to move a section of the screen. They are only appropriate for terminals. Arguments Table 1. Mnemonics for Scrolling the Display Mnemonic Explanation scroll_down Scroll down (vertical scrolling) scroll_left Scroll to the left (horizontal scrolling) scroll_right Scroll to the right (horizontal scrolling) scroll_up ScroLl up (vertical scrolling) Parameters in Output Sequence %h, %v and %x, %y —define the boundaries of the scroll window. This window can be the entire screen, a section within a field, and so on. If these mnemonics are not defined, Uniface refreshes the screen when scrolling is necessary. %p —specifies how many spaces or lines need to ‘disappear’. %q —provides how many spaces or lines will remain after the scroll. %m and %n —provide the width and depth of the window respectively. Description In the figure you see the various parameters used for both horizontal and vertical scrolling: Figure 1. Parameters with the Scrolling Function Vertical Scrolling If the output sequence for scroll_up or scroll_down does not include the %m or %x parameter, Uniface assumes that the device can only scroll over the entire width of the screen. In this case, Uniface only uses the scroll_up or scroll_down functions if the window starts in the first column and ends in the last column, for example. %h is 1 and %x is 80. When the display does not support scrolling regions and a partial scroll is necessary, Uniface refreshes the screen. If the %p or %q parameters are included in the output sequence, Uniface assumes the display can scroll several positions with one command. If neither of them is included, Uniface uses the function %p times. Horizontal Scrolling If the output sequence for scroll_left and scroll_right mnemonics includes the %n or %y parameter, Uniface assumes that the device can scroll sections of the screen horizontally and uses these functions whenever scrolling is necessary. If neither is included, Uniface only uses these mnemonics when the entire form area needs to be scrolled. If a section needs to be scrolled, the display is repainted. When scrolling, Uniface assumes that each line must be scrolled individually, and therefore uses the function %n times, raising the %v parameter by one each time. If the %p or %q parameters are included in the output sequence, Uniface assumes the display can scroll several positions with one command. If neither of them are included, Uniface uses the function %p times per line. Example: Standarad Sun Displays The following example shows the definitions used for standard Sun displays: ^027[%v;%hH^027[%pP ^SCROLL_LEFT ^027[%v;%hH^027[%p@ ^SCROLL_RIGHT ^027[%v;%hH^027[%pL ^SCROLL_DOWN ^027[%v;%hH^027[%pM ^SCROLL_UP Copy Example: Vertical scrolling: VT200 displays For example, the following definitions are used for VT200 displays: ^esc[%v;%yr^esc[%v;%hH^escM scroll_down ^esc[%v;%yr^esc[%y;%hH^escD scroll_up Copy Because VT200 terminals cannot scroll discrete portions of the screen, the output sequence does not include %m or %x . If an entire line needs to be scrolled (for example, in Zoom mode), the output sequence defines the region, positions the cursor at the top or bottom left corner of the window, and scrolls one line up or down, respectively. Because neither %p nor %q are included in the output sequence, Uniface uses this function %p times to scroll several lines. Example: Horizontal scolling: VT200 displays For example, the following definitions are used for VT200 displays: ^esc[%v;^hH^esc[%p@ scroll_right ^esc[%v;^hH^esc[%pP scroll_left Copy The output sequence positions the cursor at ( %v , %h ) and then either inserts or deletes %p spaces. Because the %p parameter is included, this function is used once per line. When Uniface decides to scroll, it calls this function %m times, increasing the value of %v by one each time to scroll each line individually. Related Links Parameters in the Output Sequence Some mnemonics include parameters in the output sequence that can be used to determine layout and formatting information, such as horizontal and vertical coordinates, numeric base zero (decimal, octal, or hexadecimal), zero padding, and so on. The output sequence can even include expressions that use the parameters incalculation, for example, to increment values. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
